## R Packages and Setup {.unnumbered}

```{r}
#| message: false
#| warning: false

knitr::opts_chunk$set(comment = NA)

library(janitor) 
library(naniar)
library(conflicted) 

library(broom)
library(gt)
library(gtsummary)
library(MASS)
library(mice)
library(mosaic)
library(nhanesA)
library(nnet)
library(rms)
library(rsample)
library(topmodels)
library(yardstick)

library(easystats)
library(tidyverse)

conflicts_prefer(dplyr::filter, dplyr::select, Hmisc::html,
                 base::max, base::min, base::sum, 
                 yardstick::rmse, yardstick::mae)

theme_set(theme_bw())

```

# Background

My data are sourced from the 2021-2023 National Health and Nutrition Examination Survey (NHANES)[^1] performed by the National Center for Health Statistics. This is a comprehensive survey that collects health and nutrition information on adults and children in the United States. It involves health exams, laboratory tests, and interviews on around 5000 participants around the United States.

I will be focusing my analysis on the nine-item depression screening called the Patient Health Questionnaire (PHQ-9) that is a part of the interview. I am interested in seeing if a score on the PHQ-9 can be reasonably predicted by other information that is collected during NHANES, and what factors impact functional impairment the most. My predictors were developed to represent depression risk factors or symptoms including difficulty sleeping or over sleeping, unplanned weight changes, a diabetes co-morbidity, and increased alcohol use[^2].


[^1]: NHANES data files for 2021-2023 can be found at <https://wwwn.cdc.gov/nchs/nhanes/continuousnhanes/default.aspx?Cycle=2021-2023>

[^2]: The depression fact sheet I used to develop my predictors, from the National Institute of Mental Health, can be found here <https://www.nimh.nih.gov/health/publications/depression>



# Research Questions


## Research Question 1

How well can you predict a subject's score on the PHQ-9 depression screening using their age, sex, average hours of sleep, average minutes of moderate activity, average number of alcoholic drinks consumed, and diabetes diagnosis in a sample of 2000 adults from NHANES 2021-2023?


## Research Question 2

What variables have the largest effect size when predicting a subject’s level of ability to function due to depressive symptoms using their age, sex, BMI, and PHQ-9 depression score in a sample of 2000 adults from NHANES 2021-2023?


# Data Ingest and Management


## Loading the Raw Data

```{r}
# alc_raw <- nhanes('ALQ_L') |> tibble()
# saveRDS(alc_raw, "ALQ_L.Rds")

# bmi_raw <- nhanes('BMX_L') |> tibble()
# saveRDS(bmi_raw, "BMX_L.Rds")

# demo_raw <- nhanes('DEMO_L') |> tibble()
# saveRDS(demo_raw, "DEMO_L.Rds")

# diab_raw <- nhanes('DIQ_L') |> tibble()
# saveRDS(diab_raw, "DIQ_L.Rds")

# depr_raw <- nhanes('DPQ_L', translate=FALSE) |> tibble()
# saveRDS(depr_raw, "DPQ_L.Rds")

# active_raw <- nhanes('PAQ_L') |> tibble()
# saveRDS(active_raw, "PAQ_L.Rds")

# sleep_raw <- nhanes('SLQ_L') |> tibble()
# saveRDS(sleep_raw, "SLQ_L.Rds")

```

First, we load all the data files we are using, turn them into tibbles, and save them as .Rds files for easy access.


## Cleaning the Data


### Selecting and Naming the Variables We'll Use

Here, for each variable, we rename it and select only the variables we need from that data file. We also change any categorical variables into factors.

```{r}
alc_raw <- readRDS('data/ALQ_L.Rds')

alc_raw <- alc_raw |> 
  mutate(avg_alc = ALQ130) |> 
  filter(avg_alc < 15) |>
  select(SEQN, avg_alc)

```

For the `avg_alc` variable, we filter to values below 15, as 15 is a stand in for all values greater than 15.

```{r}
bmi_raw <- readRDS('data/BMX_L.Rds')

bmi_raw <- bmi_raw |> 
  mutate(bmi = BMXBMI) |>
  select(SEQN, bmi)

```

```{r}
demo_raw <- readRDS('data/DEMO_L.Rds')

demo_raw <- demo_raw |>
  mutate(check = factor(RIDSTATR),
         age = RIDAGEYR,
         female = factor(case_when(RIAGENDR == 'Female' ~ 'Yes',
                                   RIAGENDR == 'Male' ~ 'No'))) |>
  select(SEQN, age, check, female)

```

We convert the original `RIAGENDR` variable to become the `female` variable, where 'Yes' means the subject is female and 'No' means the subject is male.

```{r}
diab_raw <- readRDS('data/DIQ_L.Rds')

diab_raw <- diab_raw |>
  mutate(diabetes = factor(DIQ010)) |>
  select(SEQN, diabetes)

```

```{r}
active_raw <- readRDS('data/PAQ_L.Rds')

active_raw <- active_raw |>
  mutate(min_act = PAD800) |>
  select(SEQN, min_act)

```

```{r}
sleep_raw <- readRDS('data/SLQ_L.Rds')

sleep_raw <- sleep_raw |> 
  mutate(avg_sleep = SLD012) |> 
  select(SEQN, avg_sleep) |> 
  filter(avg_sleep <= 13.5 & avg_sleep >= 3)

```

The `avg_sleep` variable gets filtered to values between 3 and 13.5, as 2 is a stand in for all values less than 3 and 14 is a stand in for all values greater than 13.5.


### Recoding Missing Values

```{r}
alc_raw <- alc_raw |>
  replace_with_na_at(.vars = 'avg_alc', condition = ~ .x == 777) |>
  replace_with_na_at(.vars = 'avg_alc', condition = ~ .x == 999)

diab_raw <- diab_raw |>
  replace_with_na_at(.vars = 'diabetes', condition = ~ .x == 'Refused') |>
  replace_with_na_at(.vars = 'diabetes', condition = ~ .x == "Don't know")

active_raw <- active_raw |>
  replace_with_na_at(.vars = 'min_act', condition = ~ .x == 7777) |>
  replace_with_na_at(.vars = 'min_act', condition = ~ .x == 9999)

```

Here we re-code any answers of 'refused' or 'don't know' to missing values.


### Creating the Outcome Variables

```{r}
depr_raw <- readRDS('data/DPQ_L.Rds')

depr_raw <- depr_raw |>
  replace_with_na_at(.vars = c('DPQ010', 'DPQ020', 'DPQ030', 'DPQ040', 'DPQ050',
                               'DPQ060', 'DPQ070', 'DPQ080', 'DPQ090', 'DPQ100'),
                     condition = ~ .x > 4) |>
  filter(complete.cases(DPQ010, DPQ020, DPQ030, DPQ040, DPQ050,
                        DPQ060, DPQ070, DPQ080, DPQ090))

depr_raw <- depr_raw |>
  mutate(depr_score = 
           DPQ010 + DPQ020 + DPQ030 + DPQ040 + DPQ050 +
           DPQ060 + DPQ070 + DPQ080 + DPQ090)

depr_raw <- depr_raw |>
  mutate(depr_func = factor(case_when(DPQ100 == 0 ~ "Not at all",
                                      DPQ100 == 1 ~ "Several days",
                                      DPQ100 == 2 ~ "Over half the days",
                                      DPQ100 == 3 ~ "Nearly every day"))) |>
  mutate(depr_func =
           fct_relevel(depr_func, "Not at all", "Several days",
                       "Over half the days", "Nearly every day"),
         depr_func = factor(depr_func, ordered = TRUE))
  
depr_raw <- depr_raw |>
  select(SEQN, depr_score, depr_func)

```

Now we deal with our outcome variables, both from the depression screening data file. We want to create a variable that represents a score on a PHQ-9 depression screening. To do this, we start by changing any values greater than 4 to missing values, as any larger numbers represent answers of 'refused' or 'don't know'. Then we filter to complete cases of all the variables that make up the depression screening score, so only completed screenings are being included. These all get added to create the `depr_score` variable. We also create the multi-categorical variable `depr_func` from the last question on the depression screening, which is not included in the score. The variable categories are rearranged into the appropriate order and changed into a ordered factor. We then select only the variables we need.


### Arranging the Tibble

```{r}
NEW <- left_join(alc_raw, bmi_raw, by = "SEQN")
NEW2 <- left_join(NEW, demo_raw, by = "SEQN")
NEW3 <- left_join(NEW2, diab_raw, by = "SEQN")
NEW4 <- left_join(NEW3, depr_raw, by = "SEQN")
NEW5 <- left_join(NEW4, active_raw, by = "SEQN")
nhanes_raw <- left_join(NEW5, sleep_raw, by = "SEQN")

nhanes_raw <- nhanes_raw |> 
  filter(age < 80 & age >= 21 & check == 'Both interviewed and MEC examined') |>
  filter(complete.cases(depr_score, depr_func)) |>
  mutate(seqn = as.character(SEQN)) |>
  select(seqn, age, female, bmi, avg_alc, diabetes, min_act,
         avg_sleep, depr_score, depr_func, check) |>
  droplevels()

```

All the different tibbles get combined into one by the `seqn` identification variable. We filter to ages between 21 and 79, because one of the questions is about alcohol use, so we only want adults 21 or older, while any ages greater than 79 are combined and listed as 80. We also filter to only subjects that were both interviewed and completed an examination. Then we filter to complete cases of our outcomes, change `seqn` to a character variable, and select all the variable we need in the order we want. Last, we drop any extra factor levels to create our clean tibble.

```{r}
identical(nrow(nhanes_raw), n_distinct(nhanes_raw$seqn))

```

We also quickly make sure that we have a unique identification number for each subject, which we do.


### Sampling the Data

```{r}
set.seed(4321)

nhanes_23 <- nhanes_raw |> slice_sample(n=2000, replace=FALSE)

```

Our sample included more than 2000 subjects, so we randomly select only 2000 of them for the final tibble.


## Print and Save The Final Tibble

```{r}
write_rds(nhanes_23, "nhanes_23.Rds")

```

```{r}
nhanes_23

```

Our final tibble has 2000 observations on 11 variables.


## Single Imputation

```{r}
miss_case_table(nhanes_23)

miss_var_summary(nhanes_23) |> filter(n_miss > 0)

```

Taking a look at the missing data in our data set, we are missing data for approximately 20% of our subjects, primarily in the `min_act` variable, with `avg_sleep` and `bmi` having less missing cases.

We will make the assumption that our outcomes are missing completely at random and they have already been filtered to complete cases. For all our predictors, we assume they are missing at random and so we will be performing a single imputation right now, and multiple imputation after selecting our models.

```{r}
nhanes_imp <- mice(nhanes_23, m = 1, seed = 4322, print = FALSE) |>
  complete() |>
  tibble()

n_case_miss(nhanes_imp)

```

After imputation, we have no missing values.


# Code Book and Description

Our final data set includes 2000 subjects with complete data on the PHQ-9 depression screening given as a part of the NHANES survey.
This was randomly sampled from our population of interest which included all subjects that were both interviewed and examined, are between the ages of 21 and 79, had an average number of alcoholic drinks consumed below 15, and had an average hours of sleep between 3 and 13.5. 

::: {.panel-tabset}

## Defining the Variables

Variable | Role | Type | Description
--------: | :----: | :-----: | :---------------------------------------------
`seqn` | identifier | \- | Respondent sequence number
`age` | predictor | quant | Age, in years, of the participant at the time of screening
`female` | predictor | binary | Sex of the participant: Yes for female, No for male
`bmi` | predictor | quant | Body Mass Index, kg/m^2^
`avg_alc` | predictor | quant | The average number of alcoholic drinks consumed on days that the participant drank alcoholic beverages, during the past 12 months
`diabetes` | predictor | 3-cat | Has the participant been told by a doctor that they have diabetes, outside of pregnancy: Yes, No, or Borderline
`min_act` | predictor | quant | Number of minutes the participant does moderate leisure-time physical activities, when they do them
`avg_sleep` | predictor | quant | Number of hours usually slept on weekdays or workdays
`depr_score` | outcome | quant | Score on nine-item depression screening called the Patient Health Questionnaire (PHQ-9). Each question receives a score from 0 to 3, then scores from all 9 questions are summed to get a final score ranging from 0 to 27.
`depr_func` | outcome | 4-cat | Final question asked on the PHQ-9 depression screening, that is not included in the score and only asked if the score is greater than zero: Over the last two weeks, how difficult have these problems made it for you to do your work, take care of things at home, or get along with people? Response categories are "not at all," "several days," "more than half the days," and "nearly every day”.
`check` | check | \- | Interview and examination status of the participant, should be 'both interviewed and MEC examined' for all subjects


## `data_codebook`

```{r}
data_codebook(nhanes_23 |> select(-seqn, -check))

```

## `describe`

```{r}
#| warning: false

describe(nhanes_23) |> html()

```

## `tbl_summary`

```{r}
tbl_summary(nhanes_23 |> select(-seqn, -check),
            missing_text = "Missing",
            stat = list(all_continuous() ~ 
                "{median} [{min} to {max}]"))

```

## Missing Data

```{r}
miss_case_table(nhanes_23) |> 
  gt() |> fmt_number(pct_cases, decimals = 2)

miss_var_summary(nhanes_23) |> 
  filter(n_miss > 0) |> gt()

gg_miss_var(nhanes_23, show_pct = TRUE)

```


:::

# Analysis 1

## Managing Data for Analysis 1

To begin my first analysis, I will be using `depr_score` as my outcome and `avg_alc`, `age`, `female`, `avg_sleep`, `min_act`, and `diabetes` as my predictors. Using my imputed data set, I select the variables of interest, along with the identifier variable, and and create a training sample and a test sample.

```{r}
nh1 <- nhanes_imp |>
  select(seqn, avg_alc, age, female, avg_sleep, min_act, diabetes, depr_score)

set.seed(4323)

nh1_split <- initial_split(nh1, prop = 0.75)
nh1_train <- training(nh1_split)
nh1_test <- testing(nh1_split)

c(dim(nh1_train), dim(nh1_test))

```

My final training set has 1500 observations on 8 variables, and the final test set has 500 observations on 8 variables.


## Outcome and Predictor Details

Now, I want to explore what our data looks like in our singly imputed training sample for the predictors and outcome.

::: {.panel-tabset}

### The Outcome

We first ensure that our outcome, `depr_score` is a count variable by checking that all of the values are non-negative integers.

```{r}
table(nh1_train$depr_score)

```

Everything looks good, so I will create a histogram to better visualize the data.

```{r}
ggplot(nh1_train, aes(x = depr_score)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 25, fill = "navy", col = "black") 

```

We see that we have a very right skewed distribution, which is common when looking at count variables. However, we have no zeros, as our outcome for analysis 2 is dependent on having a `depr_score` greater than zero, and we filtered to complete cases of that variable previously. As such, we will not consider any zero-inflated or hurdle models when we begin the analysis.


### The Predictors

For our quantitative variables, we create some numerical summaries.

```{r}
df_stats(~avg_alc + age + avg_sleep + min_act, data = nh1_train) |>
  gt() |> fmt_number(mean:sd, decimals = 2)

```

This once again confirms that we have no missing values due to imputation and that our training sample contains 1500 observations. Otherwise, all of our values look appropriate.

For the categorical variables, we create some tabyls to look at the counts of each category.

```{r}
nh1_train |> tabyl(female) |>
  adorn_pct_formatting() |> gt()

nh1_train |> tabyl(diabetes) |>
  adorn_pct_formatting() |> gt()

```

Everything looks good here as well.


### `describe`

We also run the `describe` function on the imputed training set so we can summarize the data we are using only for analysis 1.

```{r}
#| warning: false

describe(nh1_train) |> html()

```

:::

## Analysis 1 Modeling

For my analysis, I will be comparing a poisson model to a negative binomial model to see which performs best at prediction. We have no zeros in our outcome, so there is no use in trying zero-inflated or hurdle models.


### Poisson Model

```{r}
pois_1 <- glm(depr_score ~ avg_alc + age + avg_sleep + min_act + female + diabetes,
              data = nh1_train, family = "poisson")

pois_1

```

We see no warnings, so our model should have fit properly.

::: {.panel-tabset}

#### Parameters

```{r}
model_parameters(pois_1, exponentiate = TRUE, ci = 0.90) |> 
  gt() |> fmt_number(-df_error, decimals = 3)

```

Most of our coefficients show only a small effect here, but it appears the strongest relationship is between the `depr_score` and `female`, where being female is associated with having a higher `depr_score` than being male. There is also a strong relationship between the `depr_score` and `diabetes` when changing from 'Yes' to 'No', where not having diabetes is associated with having a lower `depr_score` than having diabetes.

#### Performance

```{r}
model_performance(pois_1) |> 
  gt() |> fmt_number(decimals = 3)

glance(pois_1) |> gt()

```

The Nagelkerke R^2^ is a little low here, but these statistics will be more useful for comparing models later.


#### Rootogram

```{r}
plot(rootogram(pois_1, plot = FALSE), xlim = c(0, 26), 
               main = "Rootogram for Poisson Model: pois_1")

```

Our rootogram does not look great here. The model severely under-predicts 1's and 2's, and over predicts 0's, and 4 through 9. All greater `depr_score` values are under-predicted as well.


#### `check_model`

```{r}
#| fig-height: 8

check_model(pois_1)

```

Our `check_model` results are pretty poor. The posterior predictive check is pretty awful with many of the observed data points not being included in the model predicted intervals. The over-dispersion plot is also not that great, with the predicted line being lower than the observed line. The distribution plot is not great, with an 'S' shape that indicates the data is light-tailed. The other three plots look fine, meaning we have a consistent variance, no influential observations, and no issues with collinearity.


#### Training Predictions

```{r}
pois_1_aug <- augment(pois_1, nh1_train, 
                       type.predict = "response")

mets <- metric_set(rsq, rmse, mae)

pois_1_summary <- 
  mets(pois_1_aug, truth = depr_score, estimate = .fitted) |>
  mutate(model = "pois_1") |> relocate(model)


pois_1_summary |>
  gt() |> fmt_number(decimals = 3)

```

Our calculated training R^2^ is even lower than the Nagelkerke R^2^ given by the `model_performance` function. These statistics will be more useful for comparing models later.


:::

### Negative Binomial Model

```{r}
nb_1 <- glm.nb(depr_score ~ avg_alc + age + avg_sleep + min_act + female + diabetes,
              data = nh1_train)

nb_1

```

We see no warnings, so our model should have fit properly.

::: {.panel-tabset}

#### Parameters

```{r}
model_parameters(nb_1, exponentiate = TRUE, ci = 0.90) |> 
  gt() |> fmt_number(-df_error, decimals = 3)

```

All of our coefficients are pretty similar to the poisson model. Once again we see the greatest differences in the `female` variable, where a change from 'No' to 'Yes' is associated with an increased `depr_score`, and in the `diabetes` variable, where a change from 'Yes' to 'No' is associated with a decreased `depr_score`.


#### Performance

```{r}
model_performance(nb_1) |> 
  gt() |> fmt_number(decimals = 3)

glance(nb_1) |> gt()

```

The Nagelkerke R^2^ is even lower here compared to the poisson model.


#### Rootogram

```{r}
plot(rootogram(nb_1, plot = FALSE), xlim = c(0, 26), 
               main = "Rootogram for Negative Binomial Model: nb_1")

```

The rootogram here looks better than the poisson model, however 0s are over-predicted even more than the poisson model. Otherwise, 1s and 2s are once again under-predicted, and 4 through 9 are over-predicted. The larger numbers look to be predicted a little better than the poisson model, with most of them falling within the confidence interval.


#### `check_model`

```{r}
#| fig-height: 8

check_model(nb_1)

```

The `check_model` results look better than the poisson model, but not great. The posterior predictive check is still very poor for smaller `depr_score` values, but it seems to perform better than the poisson model after that. The over-dispersion graph looks worse than the poisson model, with the lines diverging even more. All other graphs look fine, telling us we do not have any issues with constant variance, influential observations, collinearity, or distribution.


#### Training Predictions

```{r}
nb_1_aug <- augment(nb_1, nh1_train, 
                       type.predict = "response")

nb_1_summary <- 
  mets(nb_1_aug, truth = depr_score, estimate = .fitted) |>
  mutate(model = "nb_1") |> relocate(model)

nb_1_summary |>
  gt() |> fmt_number(decimals = 3)

```

All our statistics here as very similar to the poisson model, so I would not describe one as better than the other from this alone.


:::

### Comparing Models

```{r}
plot(compare_performance(pois_1, nb_1))

```

```{r}
compare_performance(pois_1, nb_1)

```

The `nb_1` model performs better than the `pois_1` model for AIC, AICc, and BIC, while the `pois_1` model performs better for Nagelkerke R^2^ and just barely better for RMSE. However, this is still on the training data, so seeing how the models perform when predicting the test data will help us make a better decision.


### Test Sample Predictions

```{r}
test_pois <- predict(pois_1, newdata = nh1_test, type = "response")
test_nb <- predict(nb_1, newdata = nh1_test, type = "response")

test_res <- bind_cols(nh1_test, poisson = test_pois, negbin = test_nb)

summ_pois <- mets(test_res, truth = depr_score, estimate = poisson) |>
  mutate(model = "Poisson")
summ_nb <- mets(test_res, truth = depr_score, estimate = negbin) |>
  mutate(model = "Neg. Binom.")

test_summ <- bind_rows(summ_pois, summ_nb) |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = model, values_from = .estimate)

test_summ |>
  select(-.estimator) |> 
  gt() |> fmt_number(decimals = 4)

```

The `pois_1` model performs moderately better in the test set than the `nb_1` model, however the results are only different by a very small amount, so I would not decide a model from this alone.


## Final Model

In the end, I chose the negative binomial model to be our final model. This is primarily based on the rootogram looking better than the poisson model and better performance in the `check_model` graphs. The predictions in the test set were so close that I do not mind choosing `nb_1` even though it technically performed worse than `pois_1`.


### Multiple Imputation

We will now perform multiple imputation to get our final model coefficients.

```{r}
nh1_mi <- nhanes_23 |> 
  select(seqn, avg_alc, age, female, avg_sleep, min_act, diabetes, depr_score)

pct_miss_case(nh1_mi)

```

We are right on the edge of missing 20% of the data, so we will fit 25 imputations to cover our bases.

```{r}
nh1_25imps <- mice(nh1_mi, m = 25, seed = 4324,
                    print = FALSE)

```

```{r}
nb_mi <- 
  with(nh1_25imps, 
       glm.nb(depr_score ~ avg_alc + age + avg_sleep + min_act + female + diabetes))

nb_pool <- pool(nb_mi)

```

### Final Model Parameters

```{r}
model_parameters(nb_pool, exponentiate = TRUE, ci = 0.90) |> 
  gt() |> fmt_number(-df_error, decimals = 3)

```

Other than the intercept term, most of our coefficients after multiple imputation are fairly close to what they were after single imputation, but some are a little more off. I believe this is due to how much data was missing in this case, as we missing data in approximately 20% of cases, allowing for more variability after multiple imputation.

Looking at the `female` variable, if we have two subjects that have the same `avg_alc`, `age`, `avg_sleep`, `min_act`, and `diabetes` values, but subject A is male while subject B is female, the model predicts that subject B will have a `depr_score` that is approximately 18% larger than subject A. In addition, our data are consistent at the 90% confidence level with an incidence rate ratio comparing A to B falling between 1.113 and 1.257, assuming both that our data are a random sample of the population of interest, and that our model is correct.


# Analysis 2


## Managing Data for Analysis 2

To begin my second analysis, I will be using `depr_func` as my outcome and `age`, `female`, `bmi`, and `depr_score` as my predictors. Using my imputed data set, I select the variables of interest, along with the identifier variable, and and create a training sample and a test sample.

```{r}
nh2 <- nhanes_imp |>
  select(seqn, age, female, bmi, depr_score, depr_func)

set.seed(4325)

nh2_split <- initial_split(nh2, prop = 0.75)
nh2_train <- training(nh2_split)
nh2_test <- testing(nh2_split)

c(dim(nh2_train), dim(nh2_test))

```

My final training set has 1500 observations on 6 variables, and the final test set has 500 observations on 6 variables.


## Outcome and Predictor Details

::: {.panel-tabset}

### Outcome

`depr_func` is an ordinal categorical outcome, so first we ensure that it is coded as an ordered factor.

```{r}
is.ordered(nh2_train$depr_func)

```

With that confirmed, we can create a table and bar graph to describe our outcome in our training set.

```{r}
nh2_train |> tabyl(depr_func) |>
  adorn_pct_formatting() |> gt()

```

```{r}
ggplot(nh2_train, aes(x = depr_func, fill = depr_func)) +
  geom_bar() +
  scale_fill_brewer(type = "seq", palette = "Reds", direction = '-1') +
  guides(fill = "none")

```

Our categories are rather unbalanced, but we have over 20 observations in each category, so we will continue.


### Predictors

For our quantitative variables, we create some numerical summaries.

```{r}
df_stats(~age + bmi + depr_score, data = nh2_train) |>
  gt() |> fmt_number(mean:sd, decimals = 2)

```

This once again confirms that we have no missing values due to imputation and that our training sample contains 1500 observations. Otherwise, all of our values look appropriate.

For the binary variable, we create a tabyl to look at the counts of each category.

```{r}
nh2_train |> tabyl(female) |>
  adorn_pct_formatting() |> gt()

```

Everything looks fine here too.


### `describe`

We also run the `describe` function on the imputed training set so we can summarize the data we are using only for analysis 2.

```{r}
describe(nh2_train) |> html()

```

:::

## Analysis 2 Modeling

Since we have an ordinal outcome, I have decided to compare a POLR model and a multinomial model.

### POLR model

```{r}
polr_2 <- polr(depr_func ~ age + bmi + depr_score + female,
                data = nh2_train, Hess = TRUE)

polr_2

```

Our model fit 3 intercepts and 4 slopes, and there are no warnings present, so the model should have fit properly.

We will also fit the same model using `lrm` to use with certain functions later on.

```{r}
d1 <- datadist(nh2_train)
options(datadist = "d1")

lrm_2 <- lrm(depr_func ~ age + bmi + depr_score + female,
                data = nh2_train, x = TRUE, y = TRUE)

lrm_2

```


::: {.panel-tabset}

#### Parameters

```{r}
model_parameters(polr_2, exponentiate = TRUE, ci = 0.90) |> 
  gt() |> fmt_number(-df_error, decimals = 3)

```

As I expected, the `depr_score` seems to be having the largest effect here, with a 1 point increase in `depr_score` being associated with a 1.460 times greater odds of moving into a worse `depr_func` category.


#### Performance

```{r}
model_performance(polr_2) |> 
  gt() |> fmt_number(decimals = 3)

glance(polr_2) |> gt()

```

I am quite happy with the Nagelkerke R^2^ here, but it will be more important when comparing models later on.


#### Classification

```{r}
addmargins(table(predict(polr_2), nh2_train$depr_func,
                 dnn = c("predicted", "actual")))

```

A total of 1109 predictions were correct, giving us an accuracy of 73.9%. All our categories are predicted multiple times, so I am quite happy with this model so far.


#### `check_model`

```{r}
check_model(polr_2)

```

Both our checks look great, showing no issues with the posterior predictive check or collinearity.


#### Validated Summaries

```{r}
set.seed(4326)
validate(lrm_2, B = 300)

```

Our validated R^2^ is 0.4896 and our validated C statistic is 0.868. 


#### `ggplot(Predict)`

```{r}
ggplot(Predict(lrm_2, fun = Mean(lrm_2, code = TRUE)))

```

We see here that as `age`, `bmi`, and `female` all have little impact on which category of `depr_func` a subject falls into. `depr_score` however, has a much larger impact, with higher `depr_scores` being more likely to fall into higher `depr_func` categories.


#### Predicting New Subjects

Now, so I can look at predictions in new subjects, I am going to create 2 subjects with the following values:

Name | `age` | `female` | `bmi` | `depr_score`
:-: | :-: | :-: | :-: | :-:
Subject 1 | 25 | yes | 30 | 18
Subject 2 | 65 | no | 20 | 6

```{r}
new1 <- tibble(
  name = c("Subject 1", "Subject 2"),
  age = c(25, 65),
  female = c('Yes', 'No'),
  bmi = c(30, 20),
  depr_score = c(18, 6)
)

```

```{r}
polr_preds <- predict(polr_2, newdata = new1, type = "p")

polr_preds_tab <- cbind(new1, polr_preds)

polr_preds_tab |> 
  gt() |> fmt_number('Not at all':'Nearly every day', decimals = 3)

```

From this, we can see that our model would predict subject 1 is in the 'Over half the days' category, while subject B is in the 'Not at all' category.

:::


### Multinomial Model

```{r}
mult_2 <- multinom(depr_func ~ age + bmi + depr_score + female,
                data = nh2_train, trace = FALSE)

mult_2

```

Our model fit 3 intercepts and 12 slopes, and there are no warnings present, so the model should have fit properly.


::: {.panel-tabset}

#### Parameters

```{r}
model_parameters(mult_2, exponentiate = TRUE, ci = 0.90) |> 
  gt() |> fmt_number(-df_error, decimals = 3)

```

Once again, `depr_score` seems to be having the largest effect here, in all categories, with a 1 point increase in `depr_score` being associated with a 2.116 times greater odds of moving into a from 'Not at all' to 'Nearly every day'.


#### Performance

```{r}
model_performance(mult_2) |> 
  gt() |> fmt_number(decimals = 3)

glance(mult_2) |> gt()

```

We are getting a lower R^2^ here than the POLR model, but it is still okay.


#### Classification

```{r}
addmargins(table(predict(mult_2), nh2_train$depr_func,
                 dnn = c("predicted", "actual")))

```

A total of 1109 predictions were correct, giving us an accuracy of 75.1%, slightly higher than the POLR model. Once again, all our categories are predicted multiple times, which is good.

#### `check_model`

```{r}
check_model(mult_2)

```

It looks like we might have some issues with our residuals here, though collinearity looks fine. For the `check_model` results I think the `polr_1` easily beats `mult_1`.


#### Predicting New Subjects

Using the same subjects as the POLR model, we will use `mult_1` to predict two new subjects.

```{r}
mult_preds <- predict(mult_2, newdata = new1, type = "p")

mult_preds_tab <- cbind(new1, mult_preds)

mult_preds_tab |> 
  gt() |> fmt_number('Not at all':'Nearly every day', decimals = 3)

```

This time, subject 1 is predicted to be in the 'Several days' category, while subject 2 is predicted to be in the 'Not at all' category.


:::

## Comparing Models

```{r}
plot(compare_performance(polr_2, mult_2))

```

From our graph, it appears no model completely dominates the other using these statistics alone.

### Likelihood Ratio Test

```{r}
LL_polr <- logLik(polr_2) # polr_2 uses 7 df
LL_mult <- logLik(mult_2) # mult_2 uses 15 df

G = -2 * (LL_polr[1] - LL_mult[1])

pchisq(G, 15-7, lower.tail = FALSE)

```

Our likelihood ratio test suggests that the multinomial model fits better than our proportional odds model.


### Test Sample Classification

```{r}
addmargins(table(predict(polr_2, newdata = nh2_test), 
                 nh2_test$depr_func,
                 dnn = c("predicted", "actual")))

```

For the POLR model, the accuracy in the test set is 75.4%.

```{r}
addmargins(table(predict(mult_2, newdata = nh2_test), 
                 nh2_test$depr_func,
                 dnn = c("predicted", "actual")))

```

For the multinomial model, the accuracy in the test set is 75.2%

## Final Model

I will be choosing the POLR model for my final model, as it is simpler while performing very similarly to the multinomial model in the test and training sets. The p-value from our likelihood ratio test does favor the multinomial model, but in-sample and test sample performance does not show substantially better performance, so I feel comfortable choosing the POLR model.


### Multiple Imputation

```{r}
nh2_mi <- nhanes_23 |> 
  select(seqn, age, female, bmi, depr_score, depr_func)

pct_miss_case(nh2_mi)

```

We are only missing less than 1% of data here, so we will perform 5 imputations.

```{r}
nh2_5imps <- mice(nh2_mi, m = 25, seed = 4327,
                  print = FALSE)

polr_mi <- 
  with(nh2_5imps, 
       polr(depr_func ~ age + bmi + depr_score + female, 
            Hess = TRUE))

polr_pool <- pool(polr_mi)

```


### Final Model Parameters

```{r}
model_parameters(polr_pool, exponentiate = TRUE, ci = 0.90) |> 
  gt() |> fmt_number(decimals = 3)

```

Other than the intercept terms, all of our coefficients after multiple imputation are fairly close to what they were after single imputation, enough that I am not worried about it.

Looking at the `depr_score` variable, if we have two subjects that have the same `age`, `female`, and `bmi` values, but subject A has a `depr_score` 1 point higher than subject B, the model predicts subject A's odds of rising into a larger `depr_func` category is 1.469 times the odds of subject B. In addition, our data are consistent at the 90% confidence level with an odds ratio comparing A to B falling between 1.432 and 1.506, assuming both that our data are a random sample of the population of interest, and that our model is correct.

### Effect Size

As my research question is focused on effect size, I want to create a nomogram and effect size graph to get a better look at what is going on.

```{r}
#| fig-height: 7
#| fig-width: 10

fun.ge3 <- function(x) plogis(x - lrm_2$coef[1] + lrm_2$coef[2])
fun.ge4 <- function(x) plogis(x - lrm_2$coef[1] + lrm_2$coef[3])

plot(nomogram(lrm_2, fun=list('Prob Y >= Several days or worse' = plogis, 
                               'Prob Y >= Over half the days or worse' = fun.ge3,
                               'Prob Y >= Nearly every day' = fun.ge4)))

```

```{r}
plot(summary(lrm_2))

```

Both of our plots show just how much of a effect the `depr_score` is having compared to the other variables. The `female` variable is having practically no effect, not even showing a separation between categories in the nomogram. `age` and `bmi` are having a small effect, but `depr_score`'s effect completely overshadows all other variables.


# Conclusions and Discussion


## Answering My Research Questions

My research question for Analysis 1 was:

> How well can you predict a subject’s score on the PHQ-9 depression screening using their age, sex, average hours of sleep, average minutes of moderate activity, average number of alcoholic drinks consumed, and diabetes diagnosis in a sample of 2000 adults from NHANES 2021-2023?

After my analysis, I would say not very well. The R^2^ was quite low and the mean absolute error of 3.348 points would be enough to put a patient into a different depression severity category. I think the more interesting question is what did our effect sizes look like for all our predictors, so we can explore what factors might cause a higher depression screening score. For example, my model predicts that, holding all other variables constant, that women will have depression scores approximately 18% higher then men. This could be explained by the higher percentage of women diagnosed with depression, or maybe there is more to be examined there about the relationship between depression severity and sex. I think one of my biggest limitations was the lack of zeros in my data, due to how I filtered it. This created issues when fitting both our models because there were so many errors created just by the models wanting to predict zeroes when there wasn't any.

My research question for Analysis 2 was:

> What variables have the largest effect size when predicting a subject’s level of ability to function due to depressive symptoms using their age, sex, BMI, and PHQ-9 depression score in a sample of 2000 adults from NHANES 2021-2023?

The depression screening score by far has the largest effect size, with a 5 point increase in depression screening score being associated with about a 6.5 times increased odds of falling into a more severe depression functioning category. The sex variable had no meaningful effect, and both age and BMI had very minimal effects. However, the predictive results of the model were promising, with a high C statistic and decent accuracy. I think I was limited here by my choice of variables, and maybe I should have chosen more specific variables that I knew impacted functional impairment in depression, instead of the pretty generic demographic information I ended up choosing. 


## Next Steps

For Analysis 1, I think there is a lot I could do better. First, I would isolate the data set for Analysis 1, so we are not filtering out all the zeros. This would hopefully let the models fit better, or allow me to explore zero-inflated or hurdle models. I would also re-frame my research question to focus on explanatory analysis, as I think it would be more interesting and probably more useful clinically to see what factors might be related to a high depression screening score.

For Analysis 2, I would also re-frame the research question to focus on prediction over effect size, essentially switching the questions I ask about each outcome. While I was happy with seeing how much of an impact the depression screening score had on the depression functioning category, it isn't exactly exciting or surprising. Since our other variables didn't have much of an impact, the results ended up feeling very obvious. I think the predictive power of this model was far more interesting, and might be more helpful clinically to predict how well a person might function using their depression screening score. Some new variables, focused specifically on functional impairment in depression, rather than depression symptoms, might improve the model's prediction ability and clinical importance as well.

The project made me realize how important it is to have a statistician working on a project from the start. Though I picked predictors that were related to depression risk factors, my first model did not perform as well as I would have liked. If this were my own research instead of a publicly available data set, the questions, and thus predictors, could have been created with my specific statistical analysis in mind. I also got a really good feel for the process of doing statistical analysis, and I am at the point where I am truly understand that a majority of the work is just cleaning the data and the analysis tends to be very quick once the data work is done.


# References and Acknowledgments


## References

1. NHANES data files for 2021-2023 can be found at <https://wwwn.cdc.gov/nchs/nhanes/continuousnhanes/default.aspx?Cycle=2021-2023>
2. The depression fact sheet I used to develop my predictors, from the National Institute of Mental Health, can be found here <https://www.nimh.nih.gov/health/publications/depression>


## Acknowledgments

Thank you Dr. Love for two great semesters of statistics, I have learned so much from you. Thank you to my family for listening to me talk about my project work even though they probably didn't understand fully.


# Use of AI

AI supported the development of this project through R Studio’s spell-check and its help with filling in the details of R functions. I also used AI search results for quick reference or pointing me to the correct websites when Googling.

```{r}
xfun::session_info()
```
